title: The Most Influential Philosophers Explained in 26 Minutes: From Socrates to Wittgenstein
description:
in Philosophy |   	May 14th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The quesôÙtion of who are the fifôÙteen most influôÙenôÙtial philosoôÙphers of all time may
body:
in Philosophy |   	May 14th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The quesôÙtion of who are the fifôÙteen most influôÙenôÙtial philosoôÙphers of all time may not arise at every conôÙverôÙsaôÙtion down at the pub ã not outôÙside the cirôÙcle of Open CulôÙture readôÙers, in any case. But even among non-speôÙcialôÙists, it could spark a liveôÙliôÙer debate than you might imagôÙine. Names likeô Socrates,ô ArisôÙtoôÙtle,ô Descartes, andô Marxô are known, after all, even among the genôÙerôÙal pubôÙlic whoãve nevôÙer read a page of philoôÙsophôÙiôÙcal text. All of them appear inô the milôÙlion-viewed video fromô JayôÙdone HisôÙtoôÙryô above, which takes its own crack at namôÙing a top fifôÙteen. Its 26 minôÙutes also proôÙvide a brief bioôÙgraphôÙiôÙcal sketch of each one, inforôÙmaôÙtive if litôÙtered with odd misôÙproôÙnunôÙciôÙaôÙtions, plus a capôÙsulized sense of these philosoôÙphersã lastôÙing ideas.
In purôÙsuit of truth,ô Socratesô creôÙatôÙed the quesôÙtionôÙing method of diaôÙlogue that bears his name.ô PlaôÙto, Socratesã stuôÙdent, advoôÙcatôÙed for rule by the enlightôÙened and the purôÙsuit of knowlôÙedge through the conôÙtemôÙplaôÙtion of pure forms. RejectôÙing PlaôÙtoãs method,ô ArisôÙtoôÙtleô dedôÙiôÙcatôÙed himôÙself to sysôÙtemôÙatôÙic empirôÙiôÙcal obserôÙvaôÙtion. On the othôÙer side of the world,ô ConôÙfuôÙciusô spread teachôÙings about the culôÙtiôÙvaôÙtion of moral virtue to mainôÙtain the social relaôÙtionôÙships he saw as the basic buildôÙing blocks of civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtionôÙal order, which ChiôÙna evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly adoptôÙed as its state phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy. Back in Europe,ô AugusôÙtineô synôÙtheôÙsized ChrisôÙtôÙian theôÙolôÙoôÙgy and clasôÙsiôÙcal phiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy, layôÙing the groundôÙwork for medieval thought.ô Thomas Aquinas, too, dedôÙiôÙcatôÙed himôÙself to a comôÙbiôÙnaôÙtion of faith and reaôÙson, makôÙing a suite of oft-citôÙed arguôÙments for the exisôÙtence of God.
SeekôÙing a founôÙdaôÙtion of absoluteôÙly cerôÙtain knowlôÙedge,ô Renûˋ Descartesô arrived at self-awareôÙness, famousôÙly declarôÙing, ãI think, thereôÙfore I amã and articôÙuôÙlatôÙing his eponyôÙmous dualôÙisôÙtic worldôÙview. Even apart from his work on the nature of knowlôÙedge,ô John Lockeãs thoughts on social orgaôÙniôÙzaôÙtion and govôÙernôÙment live on in EnlightôÙenôÙment-influôÙenced modôÙern democôÙraôÙcies even today.ô David Humeô mountôÙed funôÙdaôÙmenôÙtal chalôÙlenges to estabôÙlished ideas of empiriôÙcism, quesôÙtionôÙing our very notion that future events will mirôÙror past expeôÙriôÙence.ô Jean-Jacques Rousseauô introôÙduced the conôÙcepôÙtion of legitôÙiôÙmate politôÙiôÙcal authorôÙiôÙty as arisôÙing from the ãgenôÙerôÙal will,ã a social agreeôÙment among free and equal indiôÙvidôÙuôÙals, which turned out to be cenôÙtral to the jusôÙtiôÙfiôÙcaôÙtions of the French RevôÙoôÙluôÙtion.ô Immanuel Kantô worked to bridge the gap between ratioôÙnalôÙism and empiriôÙcism, recôÙonôÙcilôÙing the role of both expeôÙriôÙence and the mindãs physôÙiôÙcal strucôÙture to the forôÙmaôÙtion of knowlôÙedge.
Among othôÙer conôÙcepts,ô Georg WilôÙhelm Friedrich Hegelô defined that ofô dasein, which encapôÙsuôÙlates the human mode of being (and which requires a lifeôÙtime spent with his writôÙings to grasp, if even then). A proôÙfesôÙsionôÙal jourôÙnalôÙist and hisôÙtoôÙriôÙan,ô Karl Marxô described human hisôÙtoôÙry through ecoôÙnomôÙic strucôÙtures and class strugôÙgle, and his vision of a perôÙfectôÙly equal sociôÙety ahead still remains comôÙpelling to many.ô Friedrich NietôÙzscheô declared that ãGod is dead,ã placôÙing the burôÙden of definôÙing moralôÙiôÙty on man, and specifôÙiôÙcalôÙly a figôÙure he called theô ûberôÙmenôÙsch.ô LudôÙwig WittgenôÙsteinô took it upon himôÙself to explain the relaôÙtionôÙship between lanôÙguage and realôÙiôÙty with the highôÙest rigôÙor. In the comôÙments, the videoãs creôÙator teasôÙes a part two, which makes one wonôÙder which philosoôÙphers will be includôÙed:ô SpinôÙoza?ô HeiôÙdegôÙger?ô Sartre? The year and a half itãs takôÙen so far is sureôÙly long enough for the narôÙraôÙtor to have learned to proôÙnounce them.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
Free Online PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy CoursôÙes
Watch AniôÙmatôÙed IntroôÙducôÙtions to 35 PhilosoôÙphers by The School of Life: From PlaôÙto to Kant and FouôÙcault
Bryan Mageeãs In-Depth, Uncut TV ConôÙverôÙsaôÙtions With Famous PhilosoôÙphers (1978ã87)
28 ImporôÙtant PhilosoôÙphers List the Books That InfluôÙenced Them Most DurôÙing Their ColôÙlege Days
The DaiôÙly Habits of HighôÙly ProôÙducôÙtive PhilosoôÙphers: NietôÙzsche, Marx & Immanuel Kant
The 20 Most InfluôÙenôÙtial AcaôÙdôÙeôÙmôÙic Books of All Time: No SpoilôÙers
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: The Tarot Card Deck Created by Salvador DalûÙ
description:
in Art |   	May 14th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
The Tarot has long been a tool of charôÙlaôÙtans. But it has also long been embraced by brilôÙliant,
body:
in Art |   	May 14th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
The Tarot has long been a tool of charôÙlaôÙtans. But it has also long been embraced by brilôÙliant, unconôÙvenôÙtionôÙal thinkers, many of whom themôÙselves have a touch of the charôÙlaôÙtan about them (and who would just as likeôÙly admit it with a smile). William ButôÙler Yeats was a fan, as is visionôÙary Chilean filmôÙmakôÙer, artist, writer, and psyôÙchoôÙnaut AleôÙjanôÙdro JodorôÙowsky, who has recordôÙed his own Youtube series explainôÙing his take on this clasôÙsic mode of divôÙinaôÙtion. With its archeôÙtypôÙal symôÙbolôÙism, the Tarotãs appeal to artists should be obviôÙous. Most of them, like JodorôÙowsky, find farô more interôÙestôÙing uses for it than forôÙtune-telling. ãYou must not talk about the future,ã JodorôÙowsky tells us in his series, ãthe future is a con. The tarot is a lanôÙguage that talks about the present.ã
What might anothôÙer visionôÙary artist, SalôÙvador DalûÙ, think of Jodorowskyãs Tarot interôÙpreôÙtaôÙtions? Weãll nevôÙer know, but I susôÙpect he would find them enchantôÙiôÙng. Not only do the two seem like kinôÙdred spirôÙits, but DalûÙ devotôÙed some part of his life to the Tarot, designôÙing his own deck in the 70s.
IniôÙtialôÙly, the project arrived as a comôÙmisôÙsion from proôÙducôÙer Albert BrocôÙcoli for the James Bond film Live and Let Die. ãLikeôÙly inspired by his wife Gala, who nurôÙtured his interôÙest in mysôÙtiôÙcism,ã writes Chicagoãs MuseôÙum of ConôÙtemôÙpoôÙrary Art, ãDalûÙ eagerôÙly got to work, and conôÙtinôÙued the project of his own accord whenô the conôÙtracôÙtuôÙal deal fell through.ã
It was just around this time that the Tarot saw a masôÙsive resurôÙgence in popôÙuôÙlarôÙiôÙty. The occult interôÙests of the 60s counôÙterôÙculôÙture were mainôÙstreamed in the 70s thanks to books like StuôÙart Kaplanãs Tarot Cards for Fun and ForôÙtune Telling. But while DalûÙ had chanôÙneled the vivid psyôÙcheôÙdelia of the age in an earôÙliôÙer illusôÙtraôÙtion project,ô 1969ãsô Alice and WonôÙderôÙland, his Tarot deck, writes Lisa RainôÙwaôÙter at Galo magôÙaôÙzine, ãactuôÙalôÙly shows reserve. Yes, reserveãas if his revôÙerôÙence for the tarot nearôÙly humôÙbles him.ã His knack for ãfanatôÙiôÙcal self-proôÙmoôÙtionã does get the betôÙter of him evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly: he choosôÙes his own face to repôÙreôÙsent the MagiôÙcian (above).


	

OverôÙall, the deck comôÙbines the eclecôÙtic oriôÙgins of occult pracôÙtices with DalûÙãs own unmisôÙtakôÙable senôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty. DalûÙãs Tarot is ãa pasôÙtiche of old-world art, surôÙreôÙalôÙism, kitsch, ChrisôÙtôÙian iconogôÙraôÙphy and Greek and Roman sculpôÙture. Many of his recurôÙring motifs such as the rose, the fly and the bullãs head are found throughôÙout the deck.ã First pubôÙlished in a limôÙitôÙed ediôÙtion in 1984ãand reisôÙsued since in ediôÙtions by TASCHEN and in book form by othôÙer pubôÙlishôÙersãthe deck includôÙed an introôÙducôÙtoôÙry bookôÙlet that reads, in SpanôÙish, EngôÙlish, and French:
The WizôÙard (Arcanum I), SalôÙvador DalûÙ, has transôÙformed with his excepôÙtionôÙal art and his marôÙvelous talôÙent the 78 goldôÙen plates of ãThe fabôÙuôÙlous book of Thotã into as many artisôÙtic marôÙvels, each one of them duly signed by the hand of this unmatchôÙable, interôÙnalôÙly famous painter ãÎ such an extraôÙorôÙdiôÙnary artisôÙtic creôÙation does not detract, in any way, from the Tarotãs close symôÙbolôÙism. On the conôÙtrary, it enhances with its capôÙtiôÙvatôÙing beauôÙty, the Tarotãs esoôÙteric and plasôÙtic meanôÙing.
See a preôÙview video of the full DalûÙ deck above, purôÙchase a limôÙitôÙed ediôÙtion set here, or a much more affordôÙable verôÙsion here.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
AleôÙjanôÙdro JodorôÙowsky Explains How Tarot Cards Can Give You CreôÙative InspiôÙraôÙtion
The FasôÙciôÙnatôÙing HisôÙtoôÙry of Tarot Card Decks: From the RenaisôÙsance to the ModôÙern Day
Meet the ForôÙgotôÙten Female Artist Behind the Worldãs Most PopôÙuôÙlar Tarot Deck (1909)
Behold the Sola-BusôÙca Tarot Deck, the EarôÙliôÙest ComôÙplete Set of Tarot Cards (1490)
Carl Jung on the PowôÙer of Tarot Cards: They ProôÙvide DoorôÙways to the UnconôÙscious & PerôÙhaps a Way to PreôÙdict the Future
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: What Happened to Jesusã Twelve Disciples After the BibleãIt Wasnãt Pretty
description: The stoôÙries in the Bible have been told in many ways, not least through film. Among the many cinôÙeôÙmatôÙic adapôÙtaôÙtions of ChrisôÙtianôÙiôÙtyãs
body:
The stoôÙries in the Bible have been told in many ways, not least through film. Among the many cinôÙeôÙmatôÙic adapôÙtaôÙtions of ChrisôÙtianôÙiôÙtyãs holy book, none comes to mind that ends with freeze-frame title cards explainôÙing the latôÙer fate of each charôÙacôÙter, in the manôÙner of AniôÙmal House,ô AmerôÙiôÙcan GrafôÙfiôÙti, orô GoodôÙfelôÙlas. This is surôÙprisôÙing, since that device could do much to satôÙisôÙfy our curiosôÙiôÙty about so many secôÙondary BibôÙliôÙcal figôÙures. Take the twelve disôÙciôÙples of Jesus Christ, whose livesô HochelaôÙga creôÙator TomôÙmie Trelawny takes as his subôÙject in the new video above. Be warned: things didôÙnãt end parôÙticôÙuôÙlarôÙly well for most of them.
Peter, who ãhas to be one of the most studôÙied figôÙures in hisôÙtoôÙry,ã seems to have endôÙed his days in Rome. ChrisôÙtianôÙiôÙtyãs rapid spread there in the first cenôÙtuôÙry AD, evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly brought about a crackôÙdown by the rulôÙing class. The emperôÙor Nero blamed the fire of 64 on ChrisôÙtians, and Peter, now known as Saint Peter, was among the vicôÙtims of the resultôÙing perôÙseôÙcuôÙtion. Judas, the betrayôÙer of Jesus, ãremains the most conôÙtroôÙverôÙsial figôÙure in all of ChrisôÙtianôÙiôÙty,ã though quesôÙtions about his motiôÙvaôÙtions have gone withôÙout definôÙiôÙtive answers. We do know, howôÙevôÙer, that remorse evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly overôÙtook him, leadôÙing him to take his own life in AkelôÙdama, or the ãfield of bloodã ã and if you believe Dante, he now resides in the ninth cirôÙcle of Hell.
Trelawny gives the title of most underôÙratôÙed to the one whose skepôÙtiôÙcism about Jesusã return from death has guarôÙanôÙteed him his own eterôÙnal life through the expresôÙsion ãdoubtôÙing Thomas.ã (As with Peter and Judas, his idenôÙtiôÙty was solidôÙiôÙfied by aô CarôÙavagôÙgio paintôÙing.) AccordôÙing to cerôÙtain stoôÙries, he also travôÙeled the farôÙthest of any of the disôÙciôÙples: far enough to folôÙlow existôÙing Roman spice routes and found the church of the Saint Thomas ChrisôÙtians in KerôÙala, India. The not-quite-as-wideôÙly known but nevôÙerôÙtheôÙless highôÙly imporôÙtant Andrew made travôÙels of his own, going to Scythia, and from there to Greece. After his evenôÙtuôÙal capôÙture and cruôÙciôÙfixôÙion, his holy relics were scatôÙtered far and wide: even to ScotôÙland, so the legôÙend has it, home of the UniôÙverôÙsiôÙty of St. Andrews. The St. Andrewsã Cross appears as the main design eleôÙment of ScotôÙlandãs nationôÙal flag, as well as a part of the Union Jack.
In these and othôÙer ways, the legaôÙcies of the disôÙciôÙples conôÙtinôÙue to manôÙiôÙfest in familôÙiar ways throughôÙout the WestôÙern (and, occaôÙsionôÙalôÙly, non-westôÙern) world. After telling the stoôÙries of the remainôÙing eight, from John to Bartholomew to Simon the Zealot, Trelawny conôÙsidôÙers the posôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty of a mnemonôÙic rhyme for their fates. Alas, he admits, ãIãm still tryôÙing to think of what goes with ãflayed alive by ArmeôÙniôÙans.ãô ã Being a disôÙciôÙple of Jesus turns out, for the most part, to have been a callôÙing with a very low surôÙvival rate indeed. But then, in earôÙly ChrisôÙtianôÙiôÙty, marôÙtyrôÙdom was a holy act, a demonôÙstraôÙtion of devoôÙtion in imiôÙtaôÙtion of the MesôÙsiôÙah himôÙself ã and an eleôÙment sure to make most any disôÙciôÙple biopic a grueôÙsome viewôÙing expeôÙriôÙence.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
The Real StoôÙry of EastôÙer: How We Got from the First EastôÙer in the Bible to BunôÙnies, Eggs & ChocoôÙlate
The GnosôÙtic Gospels: An IntroôÙducôÙtion to the ForôÙbidôÙden TeachôÙings of Jesus
Why Real BibôÙliôÙcal Angels Are Creepy, BeastôÙly, and HardôÙly AngelôÙic
How Our DepicôÙtion of Jesus Changed Over 2,000 Years and What He May Have ActuôÙalôÙly Looked Like
How Many Lives Does God Take in the Bible: An InvesôÙtiôÙgaôÙtion into a SurôÙprisôÙingôÙly High Body Count
What Makes Caravaggioãs The TakôÙing of Christ a TimeôÙless, Great PaintôÙing?
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Watch the Moment When the Wreck of the Titanic Was First Discovered (1985)
description:
in History |   	May 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The wreck of the RMS TitanôÙic has nevôÙer ceased to comôÙmand attenôÙtion, from pop-culôÙturôÙa
body:
in History |   	May 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The wreck of the RMS TitanôÙic has nevôÙer ceased to comôÙmand attenôÙtion, from pop-culôÙturôÙal fasôÙciôÙnaôÙtion to sciôÙenôÙtifôÙic scrutiôÙny and everyôÙthing in between. That can make it seem, espeôÙcialôÙly to the younger genôÙerôÙaôÙtions, as if humanôÙiôÙty has been gazôÙing upon its remains since they first setôÙtled at the botôÙtom of the North Atlantic Ocean. In fact, the preôÙcise locaôÙtion of the shipôÙwreck went unknown for more than 73 years, between the day of the disôÙasôÙter, April 15th, 1912, and that of the disôÙcovôÙery, SepôÙtemôÙber 1, 1985. In the video above, you can watch the very moment debris from theô TitanôÙicô first came into the view of Argo, the unmanned underôÙsea camôÙera used by theô researchers seekôÙing it out.
ãSomeôÙbody should get Bob,ã says one of the crew as soon as it becomes clear, even on their low-resôÙoôÙluôÙtion black-and-white monôÙiôÙtor, that theyãre lookôÙing at man-made objects on the sea floor. And well they should have: the Bob in quesôÙtion is oceanogôÙraôÙphôÙer and Argo invenôÙtor Robert BalôÙlard, whoãd been activeôÙly thinkôÙing about how to find the TitanôÙicô since at least the earôÙly nineôÙteen-sevôÙenôÙties and boardôÙed Woods Hole OceanoôÙgraphôÙic InstiôÙtuteãs R/Vô Knorr with intent to find it.
In truth, the voyôÙage was financed by the U.S. Navy, which had much less interôÙest in findôÙing the wreck of the TitanôÙic than those of the USS ScorôÙpiôÙon andô ThreshôÙer, two nuclear subôÙmarines lost in the sixôÙties. If BalôÙlard could look for them, so the deal went, he could use the expeôÙdiôÙtionãs spare time and resources on his lifeãs misôÙsion.


	

After deterôÙminôÙing that theô ScorôÙpiôÙonô andô ThreshôÙer had implodôÙed, BalôÙlard and the Knorr crew conôÙtinôÙued on to the genôÙerôÙal area in which the TitanôÙic sank.ô KnowôÙing that the infaôÙmousôÙly ãunsinkôÙableã ocean linôÙer would have been subôÙject to the same mighty underôÙsea presôÙsure, they kept their eyes open, through Argo, for simôÙiôÙlarôÙly scatôÙtered fragôÙments rather than intact secôÙtions of the hull.ô As the video shows us, the stratôÙeôÙgy worked: only when a trail of debris leads them to an idenôÙtiôÙfiôÙable boilôÙer, proof posôÙiôÙtive that theyãd found what they were lookôÙing for, does the cheer go up. BalôÙlard would go on to disôÙcovôÙer othôÙer wideôÙly known shipôÙwrecks ã the batôÙtleôÙship BisôÙmarôÙck, the airôÙcraft carôÙriôÙer USS YorkôÙtown in 1998 ã but one susôÙpects that nothôÙing quite matchôÙes that firstô TitanôÙicô high.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: At Joy Machine, 'Feel Free' Plumbs the Tension Between Chaos and Control
description: Joy Machine is pleased to present Feel Free, a group exhibition featuring new works by Rachel Hayden, Paulina Ho, Hanna Lee Joshi, and Jeremy Mira
body:
Joy Machine is pleased to present Feel Free, a group exhibition featuring new works by Rachel Hayden, Paulina Ho, Hanna Lee Joshi, and Jeremy Miranda. The opening reception will be held from 6 to 8 p.m. on May 15, 2026.
Attempting to create order and find clarity amid chaos is human instinct. Since time immemorial, weãve endeavored to make sense of a world in which reason and certainty are never assured. Change, as the saying goes, is the only constant, which means notions of autonomy or control are a subjective fantasy rather than a concrete reality. In Feel Free, we witness four artists grappling with this enduring paradox. Each surrenders to the inevitability of change and focuses on the small instances of understanding that, for a brief moment, allow us to believe weãre closer to figuring it out.
Jeremy Miranda, ãStock Potã (2026), acrylic on panel, 16 x 20 inches
Hayden is known for her uncanny compositions that infuse flowers, plants, and insects with human emotion, often those that are unsightly or difficult to voice. Sheãs described her work as a way ãto take controlã amid situations that are so often out of our hands. For this exhibition, Hayden subsumes fruit and figures with the checkerboard pattern of a picnic blanket, utilizing color to make one indistinguishable from the other. As ants crawl along a characterãs face in the shape of perfectly arched brows, the artist gestures toward the brief intervals when disparate components align, creating an uncanny harmony.
Blending gouache and colored pencil into textured gradients, Joshi similarly reflects inarticulable experiences through her signature nude figures. In the bold ãHeld like a flower,ã the artist presents an anonymous woman with a mass of black hair as she peers down at a single flower. The thin vines mimic the gestural qualities of her fingers, suggesting an affinity between the two.
Typically working in controlled acrylic on canvas, Ho shifts to textiles sourced from a thrift shop, rendering soft landscapes with Japanese indigo. This new direction emerged from an artist residency in Joseph, Oregon, following a trip to Andalusia, where she found inspiration in the cream-and-blue Moorish architecture. Frayed edges and bold gradients capture both movement and evolution, invoking a sense of the undone and the cyclical processes that often pattern our lives.
Rachel Hayden, ãPicnic Bouquetã (2026), acrylic on panel, 9 x 12 inches
Miranda, too, captures singular moments of impermanence. Thereãs a stock pot atop a roaring flame, a bundle of plump white asparagus bound by bands, and an antique ceramic sink unadorned by backsplash or countertop. To create his painterly compositions, Miranda incorporates a wet-sanding process that reveals how ãthe painting has lived.ã Acknowledging that his own desires are not the sole factors in an artworkãs creation, he surrenders to the slippery qualities of memory and paint itself.ô 
Feel Free is on view from May 15 to June 27, 2026. RSVP.
Paulina Ho, ãSpectacleã(2026), Japanese indigo on fabric, 6.5 x 7 inches, 12 x 12 inches framed
Jeremy Miranda, ãFaucetã (2026), acrylic on panel, 7.75 x 9.75 inches
Rachel Hayden, ãSelf-Portrait as a Picnic Blanketã (2026), acrylic on panel, 11 x 14 inches
Paulina Ho, ãRestfulã(2026), Japanese indigo on fabric, 6.5 x 7 inches, 12 x 12 inches framed

title: Faig Ahmed Weaves Mysticism, Science, Technology, and Craft into 'The Attention'
description: Faig Ahmed is known for his vibrant textile sculptures that take traditional Azerbaijani ornamental carpets as starting point, often appearing to
body:
Faig Ahmed is known for his vibrant textile sculptures that take traditional Azerbaijani ornamental carpets as starting point, often appearing to melt, pool, or glitch. In his current solo presentation at the 61st Venice Biennale, where he is representing Azerbaijan, the Baku-based artist branches out into more conceptual territory, exploring science, alchemy, spirituality, and perceptions of self in a sprawling, maze-like installation called The Attention.
Curated by Gwendolyn CollaûÏo, the exhibition expands upon Ahmedãs interest in the dialectic between digital processes and time-honored, hand-crafted techniques. The artist considers how advanced scientific inquiry, such as quantum physics and neuroscience, relates to how we ãarticulate cosmologies of belonging,ã says a statement.
ãGarden of Awakeningã (2026), directional audio system
Ornamental carpets continue as a through-line in The Attention, undulating, scrunching, distending, and balling up through a series of rooms. They even extend outdoors, creating a kind of continuous runner that spills out of doorways and stretches into long lines of color.
ãAhmed bridges the 15th-century Hurufi mystic traditionãwhich viewed the universe as a coded textãwith modern information theory,ã says a statement. ãBy channeling the ãhuman energyã of the weave, he uses this ancient textile paradigm to address our eraãs information overload and collective grief.ã
Ahmed taps into a theoretical framework coined by physicist John Wheeler that can be summed up, rather enigmatically, as ãit from bit.ã Itãs a short way of describing an approach to information theory that string theorists and quantum mechanics researchers have tested. In other words, ããÎevery itãevery particle, every field of force, even the spacetime continuum itselfãderives its function, its meaning, its very existence entirelyãeven if in some contexts indirectlyãfrom the apparatus-elicited answers to yes-or-no questions, binary choices, bits.ã
In The Attention, the binaries of ãit from bitã are not only present in the way digital methods and the physical labor of the loom converge but also in Ahmedãs interests.
Detail of ãAncestorsã
ãIô have always been drawn to exploring consciousness for as far back as I can remember,ã he says in a statement, continuing:

This search has guided my attention in two directions: on one hand, toward scienceãbiology, physics, and mathematicsãand on the other, toward spirituality, art, poetry, and creative expression. At first glance, these fields appear opposite, even contradictory. One form of knowledge is directed out-ward, toward what can be measured, calculated, observed, and verified. The other turns inward, toward the subjective, the unprovable, and the inexpressible. It is an experience that cannot be confirmed or fully shared with another, just as it is impossible to truly know what it feels like to be someone else.

Merging 15th-century Hurufi mysticism with science, digital interfaces with the analog, and introspective personal experiences with objective data, Ahmedãs carpets guide visitors through the immersive space. The largest one, a monumental machine-woven piece, is titled ãI Can Contain Both Worlds But I Do Not Fit Into This One.ã It forms what the artist describes as a ãbreathing bodyã that climbs the architecture, knots itself, collapses, and spills. ãAncestors,ã a faintly anthropomorphic wall piece that glows psychedelically in black light is woven by hand. And a work called ãEntropy Altarã uses a quantum random number generator to translate visitor presence into an evolving language.
The Attention remains on view through November 22 at Campo della Tana, Castello 2124/Aã2125, Venice. See more on Ahmedãs Instagram and Vimeo.
Installation view of ãThe Attentionã
ãAncestorsã (2026), handmade wool carpet, 170 x 385 centimeters
ãThe Knotã (2026), part of ãI Can Contain Both Worlds But I Do Not Fit Into This One,ã 200 centimeters in diameter
Detail of ãI Can Contain Both Worlds But I Do Not Fit Into This Oneã
ãI Can Contain Both Worlds But I Do Not Fit Into This Oneã (2026), site-specific machine-printed carpet spanning all seven rooms
Faig Ahmed at the entrance to ãThe Attentionã

title: In 'Piercing the Veil,' Marina Kappos Gets to Know the Specter of Grief
description: Pû´re Lachaise Cemetery in Paris is perhaps one of the worldãs most famous burial grounds, home to luminaries like authors Oscar Wilde and Marcel P
body:
Pû´re Lachaise Cemetery in Paris is perhaps one of the worldãs most famous burial grounds, home to luminaries like authors Oscar Wilde and Marcel Proust, musicians and composers like Frûˋdûˋric Chopin, ûdith Piaf, and even The Doorsã Jim Morrison, among many others. Its family tombs and sculptural headstones are iconic, and when artist Marina Kappos spent time wandering through Pû´re Lachaise during a stay in the city last year, she was intrigued by the sculptures of grieving women she encountered. ãThey seemed to hold a power in their sadness, but also great beauty and remembrance as they stood guard over many of the tombs,ã the artist says.
In Piercing the Veil at SHRINE, Kapposã solo exhibition that opens this week, the artist delves into the nature of loss and memory. ãGrief is a somber subject and multi-layered; it feels fitting for the time weãre living in, but I also saw hope and life bursting through,ã she says. Few instances highlight the duality of life and death so well as the context of a cemetery, and thatãs where the artist homed in on her interest in relationships between presence and absence, the terrestrial and the spiritual, and impermanence and decay.
ãVeil Study (Eclipse)ã (2026), acrylic on wood panel, 16 x 16 inches
Piercing the Veil features Kapposã signature aura-like acrylic paintings on wood panels in which thin layers of pigment create a kind of gauzily psychedelic, prismatic effect. Consciousness is at the root of her works, reflected in the title of the show, which references the idea of awakeningãof achieving some kind of enhanced comprehension or level of perception within oneãs world. ãThese paintings depict that threshold,ã Kappos says, ãmoving from one realm to another.ã
Hazy landscapes unfold in the distance of some works, and keyhole shapes emerge almost Magic Eye-like in the center of several others. These focal portals unlock something, the artist says, ãperhaps our own beliefs and the haze of the unknown, or they can act like doorways to another time or place.ã And the figure of the widow, influenced by the gravestones, is present in elegant facial profiles and hands. Kappos likens her to someone who has not only been left behind but may be a spectre herselfãone that ãhas power, magic, strength, and can potentially straddle two worlds.ã
Piercing the Veil opens on May 15 and continues through June 27 in New York City. See more on Kapposã Instagram.
ãQuantum Study (Green Entanglement)ã (2025), acrylic on wood panel, 16 x 16 inches
ãVeil Study (Whisper)ã (2026), acrylic on wood panel, 16 x 16 inches
ãVeil Study (Sunset)ã (2026), acrylic on wood panel, 16 x 16 inches
ãVeil Study (Pink Pansy)ã (2026), acrylic on wood panel, 16 x 16 inches
ãVeil Study (Phantom)ã (2026), acrylic on wood panel, 16 x 16 inches
ãVeil Study (Aura)ã (2026), acrylic on wood panel, 16 x 16 inches
